title: Data Wrangling & Collections
beginnerExplanation: Data wrangling means cleaning, transforming, and restructuring messy real-world data into a usable format. Python excels at this with built-in string methods, list comprehensions, the csv module, and libraries like collections.Counter. Real data is never clean — it has missing values, inconsistent formats, and duplicates.
intermediateExplanation: In the real world, data arrives messy: CSV files with missing columns, log files with inconsistent timestamps, databases with duplicate entries. Before you can analyze or display data, you must wrangle it into shape. This is 80% of a data scientist's job.
advancedExplanation:
# Common data wrangling patterns
from collections import Counter

# Clean and count
words = [w.strip().lower() for w in raw_data.split(",")]
counts = Counter(words)

realWorldAnalogy.title: Organizing a Cluttered Garage
realWorldAnalogy.description: You have a garage full of random tools, old boxes, and junk all mixed together. Data wrangling is the process of sorting everything into labeled bins, throwing away the garbage, and organizing what remains so you can find anything instantly.

mentalModel: You have a garage full of random tools, old boxes, and junk all mixed together. Data wrangling is the process of sorting everything into labeled bins, throwing away the garbage, and organizing what remains so you can find anything instantly.

executionWalkthrough: 2. Scopes and local namespaces are verified.
3. Target variables are allocated or updated.
commonMistakes: Assuming data is clean — always inspect it first.
Not handling encoding issues when reading files from different sources.
Modifying data in-place while iterating over it — create new collections instead.
Ignoring edge cases like empty strings, None values, or unexpected types.

debuggingMission.scenario: An adventurer is trying to use Data Wrangling & Collections but hit a common pitfall: Assuming data is clean — always inspect it first.
debuggingMission.brokenCode:
# Common data wrangling patterns
from collections import Counter

# Clean and count
words = [w.strip().lower() for w in raw_data.split(",")]
counts = Counter(words)
debuggingMission.solution:
import csv
from collections import Counter

data = "apple,banana,Apple,BANANA,cherry,apple"
cleaned = [item.strip().lower() for item in data.split(",")]
print(Counter(cleaned))
debuggingMission.explanation: Make sure to follow the best practices: Always clean data before processing — strip whitespace, normalize case.

practiceQuestions.question: What does Counter from collections do?
options: Counts the frequency of each element in an iterable
practiceQuestions.explanation: Counter takes an iterable and returns a dictionary-like object mapping each element to the number of times it appears.

bossBattle.name: The Entropy Dragon
bossBattle.description: A chaos dragon that breathes torrents of messy, inconsistent data. You must wrangle its output into clean, structured formats using comprehensions, Counter, and string methods.
bossBattle.mechanic: Clean and analyze a massive dataset with missing values, duplicates, and inconsistent formatting.

artifacts.name: The Counter Compass
artifacts.description: A magical compass that instantly reveals the frequency of any element in any collection.

developerMode.industryUsage: Data Wrangling & Collections is widely used in standard library scripts and enterprise Python programs.
developerMode.performanceConsiderations: Optimized by standard compiler rules in modern Python environments.
bestPractices: Always clean data before processing — strip whitespace, normalize case.
Use Counter from collections for frequency analysis.
Handle missing values explicitly rather than letting them cause silent bugs.
Process data in pipelines: raw → clean → transform → output.
antiPatterns: Assuming data is clean — always inspect it first.
Not handling encoding issues when reading files from different sources.
Modifying data in-place while iterating over it — create new collections instead.
Ignoring edge cases like empty strings, None values, or unexpected types.
developerMode.architectureNotes: Interpreted and executed in the primary application call loop.
